steps.title: Identify the structural rule
steps.content: The text around the blank reveals a grammatical structure where a colon is needed to introduce an elaboration ('of keen interest to ...').

steps.title: Apply the signal to the choices
steps.content:
Structure: "of keen interest to [blank] of the University of Toronto." The blank must fill in a person. "Diane Massam" is a specific linguist—a proper-name appositive identifying which linguist.

Rule: a restrictive appositive (one that uniquely identifies the noun) takes no commas. Here only one linguist is meant, so commas would wrongly imply the name is extra information.

A. linguist: Diane Massam — a colon after "linguist" would mean everything before it is an independent clause, but "of keen interest to linguist" is not a complete sentence. Eliminated.

B. linguist, Diane Massam, — surrounding "Diane Massam" with commas treats the name as non-restrictive (optional). But she is the specific person being named; the commas imply there are other linguists and this name is just added parenthetically. Eliminated.

C. linguist Diane Massam — no punctuation between noun and restrictive appositive is correct. "Linguist Diane Massam" is a single noun phrase identifying one person. Correct.

D. linguist, Diane Massam — a comma before the name but no closing comma is an asymmetric comma that doesn't match any standard pattern for appositives or lists. Eliminated.

C is correct.
